titleStmt.title: Note from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, circa April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Note from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). The note appears to have been written on the back of company orders.
Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/211

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D1633/1/1/211 The budget will hit
us badly, as not only
do we pay 1/4 of our
income in income
tax but we shall pay
at well over another 1/4
with the excess profits
tax, as tho' our income
in some has gone
down to nearly nil, that
from others e.g. Rubber has
suddenly increased & this
Govr is taking 60 per cent
of the profits. We shall
contribute £1000 to the state
this year, but thanks to
a prb management
we can do so all right
D1633/1/1/212 Douglas not yet returned
Hope he is all right. My house might be
worse. It is a labourer's
cottage but won with
brick floors & rather
damp but I am getting
it better now. We are
in a Protestant village,
but the church has had
to be used as an office
LANCASHIRE DUMP and AUTHUILLE VILLAGE completed
17th instant. He estimates that it may take another 6
weeks before he can extend the tramway to the bottom of
THIEPVAL WOOD, and until this extension is made the tramway
is of little value to the Division, except as an alternative
route for taking stores. Stores are now being taken into
AUTHUILLE VILLAGE by transport at night and as long as this
is possible it will not be worth while unloading them at
on to the trench tramway for conveyance up
36th Div. No. G.S/5/ 10th Corps. Your G. 347 of 8.3.16. The Officer Commanding the 3rd Monmouths Company
detailed by you for tramway construction near AUTHUILLE
expects to get the portion of the tramway between
about
